patterns.what_to_look_for: Untrusted bytes (request body, cookie, header, RMI/JMX/JMS/LDAP payload) flowing into Java ObjectInputStream.readObject()/readUnshared() with no JEP-290 look-ahead allow-list, enabling gadget-chain RCE.
source_indicators: new ObjectInputStream(req.getInputStream()).readObject() / readUnshared()
No ObjectInputFilter / setObjectInputFilter / commons-io ValidatingObjectInputStream allow-list
Vulnerable gadgets on classpath: commons-collections 3.1/4.0, commons-beanutils, spring-core, groovy, rome, c3p0, hibernate
readObject in custom RMI/JMX/JMS handler, readExternal(), or XStream/SnakeYAML/Kryo with default typing
Class implements Serializable + dangerous readObject()/readResolve() side effects
blackbox_indicators: Base64 starting rO0AB or raw hex AC ED 00 05 in cookie/param/ViewState/multipart field
Content-Type: application/x-java-serialized-object
Measurable response delay on a ysoserial sleep gadget vs baseline
Leaked java.io.StreamCorruptedException / ClassNotFoundException / InvalidClassException in 500 body
evidence_required: Source-to-sink path from attacker input to readObject/readUnshared
Absence of an allow-list ObjectInputFilter at the sink
An exploitable gadget chain present on the classpath
Safe proof: out-of-band DNS/HTTP callback (URLDNS) or reproducible sleep-gadget timing delta
false_positive_checks: Input is HMAC-signed or encrypted before deserialization
A JEP-290 ObjectInputFilter / ValidatingObjectInputStream rejects unknown classes
Stream is internal/trusted only and not attacker-reachable
No usable gadget chain on the classpath
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a ysoserial URLDNS payload and confirm an out-of-band DNS lookup to a controlled collaborator host (no command execution).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report Critical RCE; include the source-to-readObject path, the classpath gadget, and the OOB DNS proof. Do not fire destructive command gadgets.

patterns.what_to_look_for: User-controlled string passed to PHP unserialize() (or a Phar:// stream wrapper on a user path) where in-scope classes expose magic methods (__wakeup/__destruct/__toString/__call) that chain into a POP-gadget RCE/file/SSRF.
source_indicators: unserialize($_GET/$_POST/$_COOKIE/$_REQUEST/...) without ['allowed_classes'=>false]
Magic methods __wakeup/__destruct/__toString/__call performing file, DB, eval, or system operations
Phar-triggering file ops on user paths (file_exists/fopen/getimagesize with phar://)
Framework POP gadgets present: Laravel/Symfony/Monolog/Guzzle/Yii (PHPGGC-known chains)
Session/cache layer that unserialize()s untrusted serialized blobs
blackbox_indicators: Serialized markers reflected in cookies/params/responses: O:8:"stdClass":, a:2:{, s:5:
Error: 'unserialize(): Error at offset' or __PHP_Incomplete_Class warnings
File-upload endpoint followed by a later file-path operation (Phar vector)
Cookie value that is base64 of an O:/a:/s: serialized structure
evidence_required: Source-to-unserialize sink path with attacker-controlled input
A reachable POP gadget chain whose magic method has an observable side effect
Missing allowed_classes=>false restriction at the sink
Safe proof: benign controlled side effect (e.g. __toString output) or OOB HTTP/DNS callback
false_positive_checks: unserialize(..., ['allowed_classes'=>false]) or JSON used instead
Input is HMAC-signed before unserialize and key is not leaked
No in-scope class with an exploitable magic method
Value is hardcoded/internal, not attacker-controlled
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Submit a benign PHPGGC chain that triggers an out-of-band HTTP/DNS callback (e.g. Guzzle/Monolog SSRF-to-collaborator), not a command-exec gadget.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report High/Critical object injection; cite the unserialize sink, the magic-method gadget, and the missing allowed_classes guard.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Untrusted data deserialized by Python pickle/cPickle, yaml.load without SafeLoader, jsonpickle, dill, or numpy.load(allow_pickle=True) — any path where __reduce__ executes attacker code.
source_indicators: pickle.loads(/pickle.load(/cPickle.loads( on request data, cache, or session bytes
yaml.load(data) without Loader=yaml.SafeLoader (default unsafe before PyYAML 5.1)
jsonpickle.decode, dill.loads, pandas.read_pickle, numpy.load(..., allow_pickle=True)
Session/queue backends using pickle (Django PickleSerializer, Celery/Redis pickled tasks)
Class defining __reduce__/__reduce_ex__ returning a callable
blackbox_indicators: Base64 cookie/param starting gAS/gAJ/gAR or raw bytes 80 04/80 02 (pickle protocol 2-5)
YAML body accepted containing !!python/object/apply: tags
Content-Type application/python-pickle or msgpack/pickle session cookie
Timing delta or OOB callback when a reduce/apply payload is submitted
evidence_required: Source-to-loads/yaml.load sink path
Loader is unsafe (no SafeLoader / allow_pickle=True)
Attacker controls the serialized bytes reaching the sink
Safe proof: OOB DNS/HTTP from a benign __reduce__ or reproducible sleep timing
false_positive_checks: yaml.safe_load / Loader=SafeLoader used
Pickle data is signed (itsdangerous/HMAC) before load and key not leaked
Data originates from a trusted internal source only
numpy.load(allow_pickle=False) or JSON used instead
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a pickle or YAML payload whose __reduce__ performs a DNS resolution of a controlled collaborator domain; confirm the lookup, execute nothing destructive.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report Critical RCE; show the loads/yaml.load sink, the unsafe loader, and the OOB DNS/HTTP proof.

patterns.what_to_look_for: .NET untrusted input into BinaryFormatter/SoapFormatter/NetDataContractSerializer/LosFormatter/ObjectStateFormatter, Json.NET with TypeNameHandling != None, or ASP.NET ViewState without a MAC/with a leaked machineKey.
source_indicators: BinaryFormatter().Deserialize(, SoapFormatter, NetDataContractSerializer, LosFormatter, ObjectStateFormatter
JsonConvert.DeserializeObject(..., TypeNameHandling.All/Auto/Objects) or $type honored without a SerializationBinder
enableViewStateMac="false", null/default/leaked machineKey, or custom ViewState handling
XmlSerializer/DataContractSerializer driven by an attacker-controlled type name; fastJSON object mode
BinaryFormatter over session, cache, or MSMQ messages
blackbox_indicators: __VIEWSTATE param present with ViewState MAC disabled, or a known/leaked machineKey
JSON body with "$type":"System..." accepted and deserialized
Base64 starting AAEAAAD/////  (BinaryFormatter header) in param/cookie
.NET stack trace: System.Runtime.Serialization / SerializationException
evidence_required: Source-to-formatter sink path with attacker-controlled payload
Unrestricted type binding (TypeNameHandling, no SerializationBinder, or no ViewState MAC)
Attacker controls the serialized blob
Safe proof: ysoserial.net OOB (DNS/HTTP) gadget callback or sleep timing delta
false_positive_checks: ViewState MAC enabled with a strong, non-default, non-leaked machineKey
TypeNameHandling.None or a strict SerializationBinder allow-list in place
Json.NET without $type, or DataContractJsonSerializer used
Input signed/encrypted and source is internal only
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a ysoserial.net TypeConfuseDelegate/ActivitySurrogate payload that triggers an OOB DNS/HTTP callback to a controlled host; avoid command execution.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report Critical RCE; identify the formatter/TypeNameHandling/ViewState weakness and include the OOB callback proof.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Node.js untrusted input into node-serialize/funcster/cryo unserialize (function eval via _$$ND_FUNC$$_), or recursive merge/clone enabling __proto__/constructor.prototype pollution leading to RCE, DoS, or auth bypass.
source_indicators: require('node-serialize').unserialize(, funcster, cryo, serialize-to-js on request data
Unsafe recursive merge over JSON.parse(req.body): lodash.merge/defaultsDeep, custom extend/deepAssign without __proto__ filtering
JSON.parse followed by eval/Function/vm.runInContext on user-controlled values
Query-string/body parsers that retain __proto__/constructor keys (old qs/express, hoek)
Payload strings containing _$$ND_FUNC$$_ or an IIFE function body
blackbox_indicators: JSON body with __proto__/constructor/prototype keys accepted and later behavior changes app-wide
Payload {"x":"_$$ND_FUNC$$_function(){...}()"} triggers an OOB callback
Global state flips after pollution (e.g. isAdmin/status defaults change for other requests)
evalmachine / SyntaxError errors from an injected function body
evidence_required: Source-to-unserialize/merge sink path with attacker-controlled keys/values
No __proto__ key filtering / no safe parser at the sink
Attacker controls the data reaching the sink
Safe proof: OOB callback (node-serialize) or an observable polluted-property effect
false_positive_checks: JSON.parse only, with no later eval or untrusted-key merge
Merge library patched, or Object.create(null)/Map used, or __proto__ keys stripped
node-serialize input is trusted/internal only
Schema validation rejects unexpected keys before the sink
patterns.suggested_validation_task: POST a node-serialize IIFE payload that performs a DNS/HTTP callback to a collaborator, or set a benign prototype flag and confirm it reflects in an unrelated request.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report High/Critical; distinguish RCE (node-serialize) from prototype-pollution impact (DoS/auth-bypass/RCE-gadget) and include the corresponding proof.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Ruby untrusted input into Marshal.load, YAML.load/Psych.load (unsafe), or Oj.load default mode, or a Rails session/cookie deserialized with a leaked/null secret_key_base — any path reaching a universal-type loader with gadget gems available.
source_indicators: Marshal.load(/Marshal.restore on request, cookie, or cache data
YAML.load( (not safe_load), Psych.load, or Oj.load(x) without mode: :strict
ActiveSupport::MessageVerifier/MessageEncryptor with a leaked/null secret_key_base (Rails cookie deserialization)
Gem gadget chains reachable (Rails 5/6 deserialization gadget, Gem::Requirement)
Ox.parse_obj / Oj object mode on user data
blackbox_indicators: Rails _session cookie with a known/leaked secret_key_base
Base64 Marshal blob starting BAh (bytes 04 08) in a cookie/param
YAML body with !ruby/object: or !ruby/hash: tags accepted
500 with Psych::DisallowedClass or 'TypeError: incompatible marshal file format'
evidence_required: Source-to-Marshal/YAML.load/Oj.load sink path
Unsafe loader (no safe_load / no strict mode)
Attacker-controlled bytes (plus valid signing key if signed)
Safe proof: OOB DNS/HTTP callback via a benign gadget or reproducible timing
false_positive_checks: YAML.safe_load with a permitted_classes allow-list, or Oj strict mode
Marshal data is HMAC-signed and the key is not leaked
Input is trusted/internal only
Rails secret_key_base is strong and not exposed
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Submit a YAML !ruby/object or Marshal gadget that triggers an OOB DNS/HTTP callback to a controlled host; run nothing destructive.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report Critical RCE; cite the loader, the gadget/leaked key, and the OOB callback proof.
